title: Lesson 441
transcript: We've now talked about how we can suffer the loss of a part like a power supply or a CPU in a system and how we can use cold spares, hot spare, warm spares. We've talked about how we can suffer the loss of a hard drive using different types of RAID. let's now move up to talking about how can we suffer the loss of an entire server an entire computer system and that's where we're going to get into clustering and redundancy so clustering redundancy is all about using multiple systems together such that if one of those systems fails You're not gonna go down. The other systems can somehow take over. But there's an important difference between clustering and redundancy. So let's start with clustering. What clustering is all about is where you're using multiple systems working together. Key word here is together. Clustering is all about multiple systems working together to support a workload. Perfect example of clustering is say having a cluster of web servers sitting behind a load balancer. So if you've got some really busy website where no one single web server on its own could handle all of the traffic, you could set up a cluster of web servers where you have say 10 web servers all sitting behind a single load balancer. And as requests come into the load balancer, the load balancer is just going to divvy those requests up amongst the cluster. And this gives us redundancy, right? Because if one of the members of the cluster fails, if we go from 10 web servers down to nine, we're still up and running. So that's clustering and how it provides redundancy because you've got all these systems working together. If one or two or three of those systems fails, you should still be up and running provided you've got enough members in the cluster. This is subtly different from redundancy. In redundancy, you have one primary system and then one or more secondary systems. And the difference is here in redundancy, that one primary system is handling the entire workload all by itself. So where redundancy is very commonly being used is in databases. There's databases that support this now, but if you go back and not to even do that long ago, trying to set up a cluster of databases would have been a terrible idea. How do you maintain consistency across all those databases? So a much more common technique would be to use redundancy with databases. And in redundancy, you'd have one primary database and all the transactions would be handled by that one primary database. What you could be doing in the background is that as changes are made to the databases, to the primary database, those changes could be replicated to the one or more secondary databases. But other than being updated, those secondary databases aren't doing anything. No users are interacting with them. Those secondary databases are just keeping up to date, but otherwise just sitting there waiting should the primary ever fail. And if the primary ever fails, then the first secondary database will take over automatically. And if that ever failed, then the next redundant one would take over. So redundancy is where you have one primary system. and then one or more secondary systems that are there and available to take over should the primary ever fail. And this leads to a really good exam question. What's the difference between clustering and redundancy from a capacity perspective? So what's the difference between clustering and redundancy from a capacity perspective if one of the systems fails? And the answer is in clustering, you're gonna have a reduction in capacity. Because if you go from say 10 web servers and one of those web servers in the cluster fails, now you're only down to nine, you've just lost 10% of your capacity. So in clustering, if one of the members fails, you're gonna have a reduction in capacity. Whereas with redundancy, you'll typically architect it so that the primary system and the secondary system are just as powerful. So if the primary fails, secondary system can take over and the secondary can support 100% of the workload on its own because it's just as powerful a system as the primary. So in redundancy, you'll typically have no reduction in capacity if the primary system fails. Okay, there you go. That's clustering and redundancy.
